2: This study analyzes the climate impact of implementing self- sufficiency systems at organic dairy farms, by utilizing the farms' own residual biomass for heat, power and fuel production. There is an increasing awareness of environmental, health-related and climate issues associated with our current dairy production pro- cesses in Europe, and more and more farms are becoming certified for organic production, eliminating chemicals and artificial fertil- izers and with increased focus on animal welfare. It is however only recently that organic labels have become concerned with energy efficiency or energy sources used at the farms. The agricultural in- dustry in Sweden uses 3 TWh of diesel per year, and still a sub- stantial amount of oil for heating (SCB, 2012). Still, environmental system analyses and the general debate of climate impact from meat and milk production are often focusing on the methane emissions from enteric fermentation in cows, and the manure management systems. Life cycle assessments (LCA) of dairy products, such as milk and cheese, typically identifies methane production by enteric fermentation as the largest contributor to greenhouse gases (Flysjoe et al., 2011; Cederberg et al., 2007; Berlin, 2002).

However, while methane production through enteric fermen- tation is an intrinsic property of the cow, and an inevitable part of the dairy production system, fossil fuels can be replaced with renewable energy sources to reduce climate impact. For example, straw boilers are today rather common at farms in Denmark and Sweden, and heat and electricity can be produced simultaneously in plants with capacity under 1 MW, fueled by solid, liquid or gasified biomass. There are numerous examples in Finland of farm- scale biogas production for heat and combined heat and power (CHP) production, even claiming self-sufficiency on heat and power (Okkonen and Suhonen, 2010). Biogas is a mixture of methane and carbon dioxide that can be produced in a relatively simple digestion process using sewage sludge, manure, or crops as substrate, and the raw gas can be upgraded to 97-98% methane which means clas- sification as vehicle fuel. There is also potential to produce for example biodiesel, ethanol or pyrolysis oil/gas from grains, vege- table oil or other biomass, that with the right adaptations and infrastructural investments can be used in tractors for field oper- ations and for transporting farm products to the markets they are aimed for. Fuel production often requires large-scale production sites; but there are exceptions, such as RME (rape methyl ester) that can be produced on-site at the farm in a fairly simple process. Moreover, while large power plants and distribution technolo- gies adapted to large-scale production (few point sources in the power production system) were built in the 20th century, today the

trend is in the opposite direction. Decentralized energy systems and smart grids for efficient power production and distribution enable individuals or industries to simultaneously be producers and consumers of energy ("prosumers") by for example adding solar panels to roof tops. For dairy farmers, becoming self-sufficient in energy would reduce cost of fuel, heat and electricity (although, obviously, with an investment cost) and might also convey addi- tional income from sales of surplus energy, in particular heat. Many farmers today struggle to survive in the harsh economic reality of the agricultural industry, and fewer and fewer are full-time farmers (SCB, 2012). That means that there are potential man-hours avail- able to operate fuel production or CHP systems on the side of the regular farm business, and perhaps a need to diversify the business in order to increase economic stability.

Studies have shown that arable farms, i.e. grain-producers, can utilize their own residual products for producing tractor fuel to cover the farm need (Fredriksson et al., 2006; Hansson et al., 2007; Ahlgren et al., 2008). The study at hand was preceded by an analysis of the potential for an arable farm to reduce its climate impact by changing from a fossil-based energy system to a biomass-based energy self-sufficiency system (Kimming et al., 2011).

This study has been conducted as a consequential LCA (CLCA) with focus on greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions resulting from the change from fossil energy for an organic milk production system to a biomass-based system for production of tractor fuel, heat and electricity. Two biomass systems were analyzed, both utilizing the biogas potential in the manure from dairy cows, supplemented with straw or rapeseed oil, to produce its entire need for energy. The amount of GHG released in each system was calculated partly based on the IPCC methodology for estimating emissions of nitrous oxide and methane from agriculture. The effect of removing straw from the fields, in order to use for combustion or digestion, was calculated based on simulations in the ICBM model (Andrén et al., 2004). The farm is assumed to be located in Southwestern Swe- den and has been modeled based on typical data for organic agri- cultural production in that region. The systems for alternative energy production are based on commercially available technolo- gies. Functional unit is 1 L of energy-corrected milk.

7: The farm studied is a virtual farm, designed to represent a typical organic milk-producing farm in Southwestern Sweden regarding size and farm activities. For simplicity it was assumed that the farm had a dairy herd of 100 cows, with 25% recruitment rate. In accordance with organic principles, it was assumed to be self-sufficient in organically produced forage, of which 50% consists of silage and grazing according to KRAV (Swedish organic label) requirements. The need for forage was calculated from Olrog et al. (2002) and the crop rotations on the farm, as well as the assumed area, were dimensioned for self-sufficiency of feed and forage. There are two crop rotations at the farm; crop rotation 1 is culti- vated over 7 X 40 ha and crop rotation 2 over 7 X 14 ha. The crops are produced in the order shown in Table 1. On the first field of 40 ha, the first year spring barley is grown, followed by three years of ley, rapeseed and finally wheat. On the second 40 ha-field the rotation starts with ley and ends with spring barley, and the 7th field starts with broad beans. According to the same principle, crop rotation 2 starts on the first 14 ha-field with spring barley, followed by 5 years of grazing and finally broad beans while the second 14 ha-field starts with 5 years of graze land. Crop yields are based on Swedish statistics for the county of Västra Götaland in South- western Sweden (SCB, 2012).

Self-sufficiency in feed and forage implies no external inputs to the dairy farm apart from fossil energy sources. Outputs from the farm include, in addition to milk, by-products in the form of meat, rapeseed oil. Straw and manure are by-products used in a circular system; straw is plowed back into the soil after the harvest of grains and manure collected in the stables is used as fertilizer on the cropland. Each dairy cow produces 1 calf/year, and 25 milk cows, 25 heifers and 50 calves are assumed to be sent to slaughter every year, which contributes to meat production (Cederberg and Nilsson, 2004). Manure is a mixture of feces, urine, bedding material (straw), water and precipitation, collected from stables to an open storage site at the farm. The grazing period in Sweden is 5 months for cows and 6.6 months for heifers and calves (SCB, 2012), and manure produced in this period is not collected. Table 2 summa- rizes the outputs at the farm with respective annual production rate.

Rapeseed oil is produced from rapeseed, with an oil content of approx. 45% of dry weight. The oil is extracted and leaves a residual product in the form of a protein-rich rapeseed cake. The rapeseed cake is utilized as feed at the farm whereas the rapeseed oil is sold on the vegetable oil market.

The energy requirement for each specific farm activity in the reference scenario is shown in Table 3. Data on electricity use is shown as total energy use during one year (Hörndahl, 2008). Spe- cific energy consumption for grain drying is 5 MJ/kg water. Truck load capacity for fertilization (manure spreading) is assumed to be 15 tonnes, and specific diesel use 0.9 l/ha during spreading and 0.5 and 0.3 1/km during transport to/from the field, respectively (Ahlgren et al., 2010). Average distance to the fields was assumed to be 1.6 km. Manure management includes mucking, stirring and pumping.

from here-on referred to as the fossil system, the farm uses con- ventional diesel, fuel oil for grain drying and electricity retrieved from the grid. The electricity was assumed to be produced in a natural gas-fired large-scale power plant.

In the first biomass-based system, from here-on referred to as the Biogas system, biogas is produced from a mixture of manure and straw in a farm-scale anaerobic reaction chamber, dimensioned to process all manure produced at the farm, with an addition of straw to cover the entire demand at the farm. The biogas produc- tion was assumed to take place under anaerobic conditions as mesophilic temperature, in a one-stage digester. The raw biogas (approx. 60% methane content) is divided into two streams. The first gas stream is led unprocessed to a gas engine stationed at the farm, dimensioned to produce electricity equaling the farm need on a yearly basis. Although dedicated to the farm requirement, in practice the power will be fed to the national grid and retrieved at the farm's actual rate of consumption, as the storage possibilities for electricity in small-scale units are limited. The second gas stream is lead via a pipeline system to an upgrading unit. This concept is inspired by the project Clean Fuel Concept in the County of Västra Götaland, that aimed at connecting several farms with biogas production units to a central upgrading facility, via low pressure pipelines (Innovatum, 2010). The main pipeline runs from the facility and through an area with high density of dairy farms, and branches out to connect to farms with biogas production. The upgrading facility uses cryogenic technology to convert raw biogas to LBG quality (>97% methane content) that can be used as vehicle fuel. In this way, farms with substrates such as manure can make use of this byproduct in a potentially profitable way. The cryogenic upgrading plant works on the principle of separating methane, carbon dioxide, water vapor and impurities at their different condensing temperatures (Persson, 2003). LBG is stored and transported in vacuum-insulated trucks, and in this study it was assumed that there is a short-term storage in vacuum-insulated tubes at the farm, although the potential is limited since there is an unavoidable leakage from the tubes and timely deliveries are a prerequisite. Heat for grain drying is supplied from a batch-fired straw boiler.
The second biomass-based system, from here-on referred to as the RME system, is based on a combination of biogas and RME

(rapeseed methyl ester). The substrate available at the farm (manure with mixed-in bedding material, i.e. straw) is used for biogas production in a reaction chamber dimensioned to handle the entire volume of manure produced. The manure itself contains a proportion of straw, in the form of bedding material. The raw biogas is used for electricity production in a gas engine, which in the same way as in the Biogas system produces the electricity required to run the milk production activities. The rapeseed oil that is a byproduct from rapeseed cake (protein feed) production is extracted from the rapeseed in a small-scale mechanical system, and passed through a transesterification process with methanol and KOH as catalyst, producing RME that is used as tractor fuel at the farm. Hence, the production system is completely farm-based and storage is also taking place at the farm. The engines can run on RME with minor modifications of the engine, such as replacing components with more resistant materials (Ahlgren et al., 2008). Heat for grain drying is supplied from a batch-fired straw boiler. Technical data and assumptions for each biomass conversion pro- cess are provided as Supplementary data.

from here-on referred to as the fossil system, the farm uses con- ventional diesel, fuel oil for grain drying and electricity retrieved from the grid. The electricity was assumed to be produced in a natural gas-fired large-scale power plant.

In the first biomass-based system, from here-on referred to as the Biogas system, biogas is produced from a mixture of manure and straw in a farm-scale anaerobic reaction chamber, dimensioned to process all manure produced at the farm, with an addition of straw to cover the entire demand at the farm. The biogas produc- tion was assumed to take place under anaerobic conditions as mesophilic temperature, in a one-stage digester. The raw biogas (approx. 60% methane content) is divided into two streams. The first gas stream is led unprocessed to a gas engine stationed at the farm, dimensioned to produce electricity equaling the farm need on a yearly basis. Although dedicated to the farm requirement, in practice the power will be fed to the national grid and retrieved at the farm's actual rate of consumption, as the storage possibilities for electricity in small-scale units are limited. The second gas stream is lead via a pipeline system to an upgrading unit. This concept is inspired by the project Clean Fuel Concept in the County of Västra Götaland, that aimed at connecting several farms with biogas production units to a central upgrading facility, via low pressure pipelines (Innovatum, 2010). The main pipeline runs from the facility and through an area with high density of dairy farms, and branches out to connect to farms with biogas production. The upgrading facility uses cryogenic technology to convert raw biogas to LBG quality (>97% methane content) that can be used as vehicle fuel. In this way, farms with substrates such as manure can make use of this byproduct in a potentially profitable way. The cryogenic upgrading plant works on the principle of separating methane, carbon dioxide, water vapor and impurities at their different condensing temperatures (Persson, 2003). LBG is stored and transported in vacuum-insulated trucks, and in this study it was assumed that there is a short-term storage in vacuum-insulated tubes at the farm, although the potential is limited since there is an unavoidable leakage from the tubes and timely deliveries are a prerequisite. Heat for grain drying is supplied from a batch-fired straw boiler.
The second biomass-based system, from here-on referred to as the RME system, is based on a combination of biogas and RME

(rapeseed methyl ester). The substrate available at the farm (manure with mixed-in bedding material, i.e. straw) is used for biogas production in a reaction chamber dimensioned to handle the entire volume of manure produced. The manure itself contains a proportion of straw, in the form of bedding material. The raw biogas is used for electricity production in a gas engine, which in the same way as in the Biogas system produces the electricity required to run the milk production activities. The rapeseed oil that is a byproduct from rapeseed cake (protein feed) production is extracted from the rapeseed in a small-scale mechanical system, and passed through a transesterification process with methanol and KOH as catalyst, producing RME that is used as tractor fuel at the farm. Hence, the production system is completely farm-based and storage is also taking place at the farm. The engines can run on RME with minor modifications of the engine, such as replacing components with more resistant materials (Ahlgren et al., 2008). Heat for grain drying is supplied from a batch-fired straw boiler. Technical data and assumptions for each biomass conversion pro- cess are provided as Supplementary data.
17: Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a method for analyzing the envi- ronmental impact using a wide range of impact categories for products and services. LCA is standardized in ISO 14040 (CEN, 2006) and the methodology is continuously evolving; currently there are two main versions, the consequential (CLCA) and the attributional LCA (ACLA). The CLCA approach is used here as this study is investigating a change from fossil to renewable energy systems. In a CLCA, the environmental impact stemming from the change to a reference system is measured and marginal data on for example electricity use applies (Curran et al., 2005). It is to be used with attention to a high degree of transparency to methodological choices and assumptions, due to the wide range of results that can be obtained from LCAs, even when conducted over identical products (Gnansounou et al., 2009). In CLCA allocation is avoided by so called substitution, meaning that processes that are avoided as a consequence of useful co-products from the system are considered avoided burdens, and the impacts are subtracted from the burden of the system studied.
18: <header>2.4. System boundaries, substituted products and functional unit </header>

The system boundary is the farm-gate for farm outputs, but activities for farm-based energy supply systems that take place outside the farm (such as biogas upgrading) are included. Residual products from the conversion processes, such as ashes and diges- tion residues, were assumed to be returned to the soil as fertilizers. By-products, such as meat from the milk production system, have been considered by applying the substitution method. All three systems are schematically depicted in Fig. 1a-c. Surplus electricity production capacity added in the Biogas and RME systems are assumed to replace production from long-term marginal production capacity, the capacity that is assumed to be built in response to a higher market demand, or decommissioned based on a reduced demand (Lund et al., 2010). This was assumed to be based on natural gas, given the projected commissioning and decommissioning of power plants in Europe over the next 30 years (Kjaerstad and Jonsson, 2007). It is important to separate the short- term marginal production (daily or seasonal fluctuations) with long-term (or built) marginal production - only the latter applies in this case (Mathiesen et al., 2009). The same type of marginal electricity production is assumed to be replaced from surplus electricity production in the biomass-based systems. Surplus fuel production is assumed to replace diesel.
Fig. 1. a-c. The Fossil reference, Biogas system and Biogas + RME systems. Energy production in the renewable systems include biogas and RME production and upgrading.

A cow gives 259 kg bone-free meat at slaughter and a calf 80 kg (Cederberg and Nilsson, 2004). Meat from surplus calves and non- productive cows substitutes beef production, which is a separate production system. Previous LCA studies have identified a substi- tution factor of 5.6 g meat per kg of ECM (Cederberg and Nilsson, 2004) and the GHG emissions for beef production systems 22 kg CO2-eq./kg meat (Cederberg et al., 2011). Emissions from external rapeseed production in the RME system (replacing oil used for RME at the farm) were 0.8 kg CO2-eq./kg oil based on data in the literature (Cederberg et al., 2011).

Capital goods needed in conversion from fossil to renewable energy systems, such as the anaerobic digester, engines and boilers were included, using data from Ecoinvent (2003). The lifetime of the equipment and machinery was set to 20 years, and that of pipelines for raw gas delivery at 30 years. In the system in which energy to the farm is produced with fossil fuels, the mining, extraction, refining, distribution and consumption of the fuels were included.

21: The functional unit in this study is 1 kg of energy-corrected milk (ECM). Impact categories are GWP and energy use. Emissions fac- tors used are 23 kg CO2-eq./kg for methane and 298 kg CO2-eq./kg for nitrous oxide.

Methane emissions from storage of manure and digestion resi- dues were calculated according to the IPCC Guidelines (IPCC, 2006). The fraction of methane volatized was set at 10% of maximum methane production capacity (Bo) based on IPCC guidelines for liquid manure (IPCC, 2006). BO for digested residues and manure was set at 0.012 m3/kg volatile solids (VS) and 0.25 n3/kg VS, respectively (Edstrom et al., 2008).

Methane is formed in the digestive system of dairy cows when cellulose is decomposed via anaerobic microbial activity and de- pends on milk production rate, feed and the cow's body weight. The emissions were calculated using Eq. (1): (Kirchgessner et al., 1991)
CH4(g/day) = 55 + 4.5 X (kg milk/cow and day) + 1.2 (1) metabolic weight
where metabolic weight = (body weight) 0.75 For a dairy cow producing 8000 kg milk/yr (with 84% avail- ability), assuming a metabolic weight of 600 kg/cow, the emissions
are 120 kg CH4/COW and yr. For heifers, 50 kg CH4/animal and yr was assumed (Cederberg et al., 2007).

Direct emissions of nitrous oxide (N20) from soil and fertilizers were calculated based on IPCC methodology, i.e. 1% of added ni- trogen and 1% of nitrogen content in crop residues volatize as nitrous oxide. Data on crop residues left on the fields and on ni- trogen content in different crops were taken from the IPCC Guidelines.

Indirect emissions in the form of leached nitrogen (which volatizes to N20 downstream) were set to 0.75% of leaching (IPCC, 2006) and the amount leached was assumed to be 11 kg N/ha (Wivstad et al., 2009). The amount of applied nitrogen that vola- tizes as ammonia and later re-deposits was assumed to be 1.2% of applied fertilizer based on common Swedish fertilization practices (Ahlgren et al., 2009) and the N20 emissions factor of the re- deposited nitrogen was set at 1% (IPCC, 2006). Emissions of N20 from storage of manure and digestion residues were set at 0.1% of the nitrogen content available in the substrate, based on emission factors for liquid manure (IPCC, 2006).

24: The ICBM model (Andrén et al., 2004) was used for the simu- lation of carbon flows in the soil under the cultivation systems studied. The model assumes two different carbon pools in the soil, the young pool Y, and the old pool O, where C entering the soil first belongs to the unstable young pool where it easily is oxidized to CO2, whereas it at a certain rate, depending on its properties, will become humified and hence belong to the old pool, which is rela- tively stable. Eqs. (2a) and (2b) describe the steady state (ss) of the young and old pool, respectively:
Yss = i/kyr (2a) Oss = hi/kor (2b) and Eqs. (3a) and (3b) describe the carbon flows: dY/dt = i - kyreY (3a) do/dt = hkyreY - koreO (3b)

where i is the input of C from crop residues, digestion residues or manure (C input in kg/m2 of respective material, here calculated for each scenario), ky (0.8) and ko (0.006) are constants reflecting the stability of the respective pools, Y and o are concentration of C in the Young and the Old pool (kg/m²), re depends on external in- fluences such as soil climate, crop type and frequency of soil tillage (for example, ley has a lower value than annual crops due because it requires less tillage), and h is the humification coefficient, i.e. the fraction of Y that is transferred to O. Digestion residues and manure are according to the model humidified to a larger extent than straw, and hence have higher h (h = 0.47, 0.31 and 0.12 for digestion residues, manure and straw, respectively). The result of the model is the annual oxidization of carbon to CO2 from the soil, calculated as an average over 21 years (three crop rotations).

28: GHG emissions are dominated by emissions from enteric fermentation in all scenarios (Fig. 2) followed by nitrous oxide from soil, methane from manure storage and energy carrier production (in the fossil scenario). However, while in the fossil system enteric fermentation constitute 50%, it is 90% and 70% of the emissions in the Biogas and RME systems, respectively, while GHG emissions related to the energy system in the Fossil system are 25% of total emissions, 5% in the Biogas system and 3% in the RME system (mainly stemming from the production of engines and boilers).

There is a reduction of CO2 emissions from cropland in the Biogas system, a consequence of a larger straw content in the biogas substrate; the processing of straw and manure through the anaer- obic digester produces a material that is more stable and moves into the more stable carbon pool. Moreover, the emissions from manure management are almost eliminated in both the Biogas and the RME systems due to the collection and use of methane from manure.

Total reduction of GHG emissions are 46% in the Biogas system and 32% in the RME system (Table 6). The reduction of GHG emissions isolated to only include parameters that are altered due to a change in energy system (for example, excluding enteric fermentation) is 94% and 70%, respectively.

Parameters considered particularly uncertain or of high impor- tance were altered in order to determine the sensitivity of the re- sults (Table 7). Only parameters that could have different impact on the different scenarios were changed.

The assumptions on electricity production technologies are often critical in CLCA, as it often influences the results strongly. There is no standard methodology for identifying marginal elec- tricity capacity taken into production (or commissioned) as a response to increase in demand (Curran et al., 2005). We assumed the marginal electricity to be high-efficiency natural gas-fired condensing plants on the basis that it has been the most common technology for new-built electricity production capacity in Europe for a long time (Kjaerstad and Jonsson, 2007). The price of natural gas is currently on historically low levels, triggering an interest in the technology, although wind has in fact recently surpassed nat- ural gas-fueled condensing plants as the production technology with the fastest rate of growth in Europe, 12.6% in 2012 (EWEA, 2013). Coal is in the sensitivity analysis used as an example of high-emission production and wind as low-emission production.

Table 7 shows that in particular emission rates of methane from manure storages, and assumption of power source are important. Both parameters can be difficult to establish. Methodologies and empirical studies available today are not sufficiently site-specific to ensure a completely correct result on emission rates. Power source in CLCA is marginal capacity, and not straightforward to deduct. Calculations have been done with the best available data and models today.

37: The GHG emissions from the reference system obtained in this study are close to results in previous LCA studies of milk (Cederberg et al., 2007; Thomassen et al., 2008). This study also includes GHG emissions from completely renewable energy systems, which have not been found in the literature. The study shows that the farm not only could become self-sufficient in energy, but that the potential emission reductions from milk production are substantial. An

important parameter was the changes in soil-C added or removed from the soil based on manure management systems; anaerobic treatment (digestion) or no treatment (spreading fresh manure and straw), for which the ICBM model was used. The model has been validated by field experiments (Kätterer and Andrén, 1999), but in this study it has adapted to the crop rotations developed theoret- ically and hence, no validation of the specific simulation for this case has been done. The model assumes two carbon pools, which obviously is a simplification; a field of fertile soil is likely to consist of several different "pools" where added organic matter become humified (creating a 'carbon sink') at different rates. In this study the difference in soil-C sequestration is small between the Fossil system (returning fresh manure to the soil) and the RME system (returning digested manure to the soil), whereas CO2-release is reduced considerably when straw is anaerobically treated (diges- ted) rather than plowed down fresh. This is a function of the low humification coefficient of straw, resulting in a larger amount of easily oxidized C. However, in fact experimental results on the stabilization of C in soil show different results; whereas one study found that the percentage of CO2-C released from digested cattle manure was higher than for non-treated or aerobically treated (composted) manure (Kirchmann and Bernal, 1997), another (long- lying) study suggests that digested manure increase soil organic matter compared to non-treated or aerobically treated manure, in part because of higher yields the years to follow due to an improved N-fertilization effect (Rauhe and Hesse, 1960). The better N-fertil- ization achieved when spreading of digestion residues on the fields rather than plowing down crop residues is a consequence of higher precision in time and space of N-addition (Möller and Stinner, 2009).

Effects of indirect land use changes (iLUC), should be taken into account when there is a possibility that a production activity, for example production of biofuels, will push aside food production from agricultural land somewhere. When this happens, it can be assumed to drive conversion of other land to agricultural fields in order to meet global food demand which is rather inelastic. It is not easy to determine where and which type of land that would be converted, but if it conveys deforestation it causes a pulse of greenhouse gases to the atmosphere. The converted forest is either burnt (instant addition of CO2) or mulched (CO2 added to the at- mosphere over a long period of time) (Börjesson and Ahlgren, 2011). Moreover, soil on agricultural fields is more exposed than soil in forests, which means that more of the soil-C mineralizes to CO2 and escape to the atmosphere. The iLUC issue is to an increasing extent considered in LCA's; research has been conducted on how to quantify this effect and attribute it to agricultural products, but there is no general consensus amongst the research society and policymakers today on an appropriate methodology (Gawel and Ludwig, 2011). An iLUC factor would be applicable for this study as rapeseed oil was removed from the market as in the RME system, and will be produced somewhere else, on some other field - potentially triggering a chain of substituted production that concludes with demand for new agricultural land (Kloverpris, 2008). It has however been omitted, partly because it was deemed to have a small impact on the total results, and partly because it is very difficult to estimate. Apart from rapeseed oil, the biomass resources used in the renewable energy scenarios were residues that do not cause any iLUC effect.

There are barriers to the implementation of renewable energy systems at farms, not least high investment cost and lack of knowledge and experience in how to operate and maintain the systems in an economically optimized way. A system with an external upgrading facility for biogas requires high investments and involves risk, and most likely it would have to be a joint venture between private investors, municipalities or other public

funding, and the risk reduced by policy instruments such as feed- in tariffs or tax relieves. Today not even farm-scale biogas units are economically viable at a farm size equal to the farm in this study, in Sweden. However, the number of units in Sweden has increased in the past few years due to government support for investment in biogas production from manure. Although the feedstock can be considered to be "for free" (except for rapeseed oil and straw that can be sold on a market and hence have an opportunity cost), the systems require large investments in con- version technology and infrastructure, such as pipelines and storage systems. The biogas systems in this study are however sub-optimized as waste heat is redundant but not put to use. It would be rational and make economic sense to find a heat sink, for example a greenhouse or small industry with willingness to pay for it. The CHP systems produce about 800 GJ heat per year, but only 200-250 GJ are used at the farm. That leaves 550 GJ, enough to heat 10 regular homes. Given a raw price for heating of 0.5 SEK/kWh heat in a district heating system, this is a potential income of 000 SEK/yr for the farmer. Another potential use for the surplus heat is in absorption cooling systems for milk cooling (Brown and Pettersson, 2009). Moreover, in the Biogas system there is a surplus of 3500 Nm³ LBG and in the RME system there is a surplus of 35 MWh of electricity that can be sold to respective market.

The results also show that GHG emissions from milk production are dominated by emissions from enteric fermentation; in the reference system they constitute about 50% of total emissions whereas in the Biogas and RME systems, the enteric emissions share of emissions rise to 90% and 70%, respectively. Research is ongoing on how to reduce such emissions by for example changing the cows' diet. The results of this study indicate that such research can become the key to further reducing the climate impact of dairy production.

Effects of indirect land use changes (iLUC), should be taken into account when there is a possibility that a production activity, for example production of biofuels, will push aside food production from agricultural land somewhere. When this happens, it can be assumed to drive conversion of other land to agricultural fields in order to meet global food demand which is rather inelastic. It is not easy to determine where and which type of land that would be converted, but if it conveys deforestation it causes a pulse of greenhouse gases to the atmosphere. The converted forest is either burnt (instant addition of CO2) or mulched (CO2 added to the at- mosphere over a long period of time) (Börjesson and Ahlgren, 2011). Moreover, soil on agricultural fields is more exposed than soil in forests, which means that more of the soil-C mineralizes to CO2 and escape to the atmosphere. The iLUC issue is to an increasing extent considered in LCA's; research has been conducted on how to quantify this effect and attribute it to agricultural products, but there is no general consensus amongst the research society and policymakers today on an appropriate methodology (Gawel and Ludwig, 2011). An iLUC factor would be applicable for this study as rapeseed oil was removed from the market as in the RME system, and will be produced somewhere else, on some other field - potentially triggering a chain of substituted production that concludes with demand for new agricultural land (Kloverpris, 2008). It has however been omitted, partly because it was deemed to have a small impact on the total results, and partly because it is very difficult to estimate. Apart from rapeseed oil, the biomass resources used in the renewable energy scenarios were residues that do not cause any iLUC effect.

There are barriers to the implementation of renewable energy systems at farms, not least high investment cost and lack of knowledge and experience in how to operate and maintain the systems in an economically optimized way. A system with an external upgrading facility for biogas requires high investments and involves risk, and most likely it would have to be a joint venture between private investors, municipalities or other public

funding, and the risk reduced by policy instruments such as feed- in tariffs or tax relieves. Today not even farm-scale biogas units are economically viable at a farm size equal to the farm in this study, in Sweden. However, the number of units in Sweden has increased in the past few years due to government support for investment in biogas production from manure. Although the feedstock can be considered to be "for free" (except for rapeseed oil and straw that can be sold on a market and hence have an opportunity cost), the systems require large investments in con- version technology and infrastructure, such as pipelines and storage systems. The biogas systems in this study are however sub-optimized as waste heat is redundant but not put to use. It would be rational and make economic sense to find a heat sink, for example a greenhouse or small industry with willingness to pay for it. The CHP systems produce about 800 GJ heat per year, but only 200-250 GJ are used at the farm. That leaves 550 GJ, enough to heat 10 regular homes. Given a raw price for heating of 0.5 SEK/kWh heat in a district heating system, this is a potential income of 000 SEK/yr for the farmer. Another potential use for the surplus heat is in absorption cooling systems for milk cooling (Brown and Pettersson, 2009). Moreover, in the Biogas system there is a surplus of 3500 Nm³ LBG and in the RME system there is a surplus of 35 MWh of electricity that can be sold to respective market.

The results also show that GHG emissions from milk production are dominated by emissions from enteric fermentation; in the reference system they constitute about 50% of total emissions whereas in the Biogas and RME systems, the enteric emissions share of emissions rise to 90% and 70%, respectively. Research is ongoing on how to reduce such emissions by for example changing the cows' diet. The results of this study indicate that such research can become the key to further reducing the climate impact of dairy production.

43: The results of this study show that an organic dairy farm can become self-sufficient in energy by using its own residual resources in the form of manure, straw and vegetable oil. The GHG emissions from production of 1 kg ECM was shown to be reduced by 46% by implementing the energy system based on manure and straw, or 32% by implementing a system based on manure, straw and rape- seed oil. Besides the elimination of emissions from fossil fuel use, GHG emission reductions are achieved by altering the manure management system, affecting both CO2, NH4 and N20 release from soil and fertilizer.

For the farmer, the renewable energy systems can be a way to reduce the climate impact from milk production as a competitive advantage. The cost of diesel and external heat and electricity is eliminated by renewable systems based on own resources, but the investment cost of these systems, and the required advanced upgrading facility in the Biogas system, still constitute high barriers to implementation.
